perturbation.original_text: Either Party may disclose Confidential Information to the other Party in confidence provided that the disclosing Party identifies such information as proprietary and confidential either by marking it, in the case of written materials, or, in the case of information that is disclosed orally or written materials that are not marked, by notifying the other Party of the proprietary and confidential nature of the information, such notification to be done orally, by email or written correspondence, or via other means of communication as might be appropriate.
perturbation.changed_text: Either Party may disclose Confidential Information to the other Party in confidence provided that the disclosing Party identifies such information as proprietary and confidential either by marking it, in the case of written materials, or, in the case of information that is disclosed orally or written materials that are not marked, by notifying the other Party of the proprietary and confidential nature of the information, such notification to be done orally, by email and not written correspondence, or via other means of communication as might be appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: The change restricts notification of confidential information, for unmarked written materials or information disclosed orally, to oral or email communication only, removing 'written correspondence' as a valid method. This contradicts the broad communication means indicated by 'other means of communication as might be appropriate,' creating uncertainty about the validity of written notifications.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Either Party may disclose Confidential Information to the other Party in confidence provided that the disclosing Party identifies such information as proprietary and confidential either by marking it, in the case of written materials, or, in the case of information that is disclosed orally or written materials that are not marked, by notifying the other Party of the proprietary and confidential nature of the information, such notification to be done orally, by email and not written correspondence, or via other means of communication as might be appropriate.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes 'written correspondence' as a valid method for notifying the other party of confidential information. The contradicted text, which is identical to the changed text, includes the phrase 'other means of communication as might be appropriate.' This introduces a contradiction because while 'other means of communication as might be appropriate' implies a broad range of options, including written correspondence, the explicit exclusion of 'written correspondence' earlier in the same clause contradicts this broader statement. A reasonable reader would recognize the tension between the explicit exclusion and the general inclusion, creating a strong in-text contradiction as per the definition of 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient shall ensure that each of its employees, officers, directors, or agents who has access to Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement is informed of its proprietary and confidential nature and is required to abide by the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient shall ensure that only its officers and directors who has access to Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement is informed of its proprietary and confidential nature and is required to abide by the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The changed text limits the scope of who needs to be informed about the confidential nature of the information only its officers and directors, removing the requirement for employees and agents. This contradicts the earlier statement defining the receiving party which includes a wider array of people.
perturbation.contradicted_text: When informed of the proprietary and confidential nature of Confidential Information that has been disclosed by the other Party, the receiving Party ("Recipient") shall, for a period of one (1) year from the date of disclosure, refrain from disclosing such Confidential Information to any contractor or other third party without prior, written approval from the disclosing Party and shall protect such Confidential Information from inadvertent disclosure to a third party using the same care and diligence that the Recipient uses to protect its own proprietary and confidential information, but in no case less than reasonable care. The Recipient shall ensure that only its officers and directors who has access to Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement is informed of its proprietary and confidential nature and is required to abide by the terms of this Agreement. The Recipient of Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement shall promptly notify the disclosing Party of any disclosure of such Confidential Information in violation of this Agreement or of any subpoena or other legal process requiring production or disclosure of said Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text narrows the scope of who needs to be informed about confidential information to only officers and directors, while the original text included employees and agents. The contradicted text includes the changed text and because of that change, there is now a contradiction between the people that the receiving party ('Recipient') needs to inform when handling confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding the above, the Parties agree that information shall not be deemed Confidential Information and the Recipient shall have no obligation to hold in confidence such information, where such information: (a) Is already known to the Recipient, having been disclosed to the Recipient by a third party without such third party having an obligation of confidentiality to the disclosing Party; or (b) Is or becomes publicly known through no wrongful act of the Recipient, its employees, officers, directors, or agents; or (c) Is independently developed by the Recipient without reference to any Confidential Information disclosed hereunder; or (d) Is approved for release (and only to the extent so approved) by the disclosing Party; or (e) Is disclosed pursuant to the lawful requirement of a court or governmental agency or where required by operation of law.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding the above, the Parties agree that information shall not be deemed Confidential Information and the Recipient shall have no obligation to hold in confidence such information, where such information: (a) Is already known to the Recipient; or (b) Is or becomes publicly known; or (c) Is independently developed by the Recipient; or (d) Is approved for release (and only to the extent so approved) by the disclosing Party; or (e) Is disclosed pursuant to the lawful requirement of a court or governmental agency or where required by operation of law.
perturbation.explanation: This removes the condition that the third party disclosing the information to the Recipient must have no obligation of confidentiality to the disclosing Party (a). It also removes the clause 'through no wrongful act of the Recipient, its employees, officers, directors, or agents' (b) and the clause 'without reference to any Confidential Information disclosed hereunder' (c). Now any information known, public, or independently developed is not confidential regardless of the origin. This contradicts the entire premise of the NDA as any leaked information could be considered not confidential.
perturbation.contradicted_text: All Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement shall be and remain the property of the disclosing Party and nothing contained in this Agreement shall be construed as granting or conferring any rights to such Confidential Information on the other Party. The Recipient shall honor any request from the disclosing Party to promptly return or destroy all copies of Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement and all notes related to such Confidential Information. The Parties agree that the disclosing Party will suffer irreparable injury if its Confidential Information is made public, released to a third party, or otherwise disclosed in breach of this Agreement and that the disclosing Party shall be entitled to obtain injunctive relief against a threatened breach or continuation of any such breach and, in the event of such breach, an award of actual and exemplary damages from any court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly broadens the exceptions to what is considered Confidential Information. By removing the requirements that the third party disclosing the information has no confidentiality obligation, that the information didn't become public through wrongful acts of the Recipient, and that the independent development didn't reference confidential information, the changed text allows almost any information to be considered non-confidential. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which states that 'All Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement shall be and remain the property of the disclosing Party' and that the disclosing party will suffer irreparable injury if its Confidential Information is made public, released to a third party, or otherwise disclosed in breach of this Agreement. Since the Changed Text creates inconsistencies between these two clauses, the answer is YES.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement contains the entire agreement between the Parties and in no way creates an obligation for either Party to disclose information to the other Party or to enter into any other agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement contains the entire agreement between the Parties and creates an obligation for both party to disclose all confidential information if requested by the other Party.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text contradicts the original agreement by creating an obligation to disclose all confidential information if requested by the other party. This in-text perturbation now creates an obligation that did not exist previously and directly contradicts the earlier statement that 'in no way creates an obligation for either Party to disclose information to the other Party'. This change undermines the fundamental principle of voluntary disclosure that the agreement was built upon, causing uncertainty and legal disputes
perturbation.contradicted_text: This Agreement contains the entire agreement between the Parties and creates an obligation for both party to disclose all confidential information if requested by the other Party.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly states that the agreement 'in no way creates an obligation for either Party to disclose information to the other Party'. The changed text directly contradicts this by stating that the agreement 'creates an obligation for both party to disclose all confidential information if requested by the other Party'. This is a direct conflict within the agreement, undermining the principle of voluntary disclosure. Therefore, according to the definition of inconsistencies, a strong in-text contradiction exists, and the contradictory text provided is the Changed Text itself.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient shall honor any request from the disclosing Party to promptly return or destroy all copies of Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement and all notes related to such Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient shall honor any request from the disclosing Party to promptly return all copies of Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement but not the notes related to such Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text contradicts the original agreement by creating a rule that says the Recipient doesn't need to honor a request for the disclosing Party to return the notes about the confidential information disclosed during the agreement. This in-text perturbation now creates a loophole because there is no longer an obligation to return such information, causing uncertainty and legal disputes.
perturbation.contradicted_text: All Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement shall be and remain the property of the disclosing Party and nothing contained in this Agreement shall be construed as granting or conferring any rights to such Confidential Information on the other Party. The Recipient shall honor any request from the disclosing Party to promptly return all copies of Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement but not the notes related to such Confidential Information. The Parties agree that the disclosing Party will suffer irreparable injury if its Confidential Information is made public, released to a third party, or otherwise disclosed in breach of this Agreement and that the disclosing Party shall be entitled to obtain injunctive relief against a threatened breach or continuation of any such breach and, in the event of such breach, an award of actual and exemplary damages from any court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly states that the Recipient does not need to return notes related to confidential information. The contradicted text includes the changed text and emphasizes the disclosing Party's ownership and right to all Confidential Information and indicates that the Recipient should honor any request from the disclosing Party to promptly return all copies of Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement but not the notes related to such Confidential Information. While the first part of the sentence says the Recipient shall honor any request to return copies of Confidential Information, the change causes a contradiction by explicitly excluding "notes." This creates an internal contradiction within the same sentence, impacting enforceability and clarity.
